title: Blood on Carpet
stainChemistry: Blood on carpet is the same protein chemistry as on fabric — hemoglobin, fibrin, albumin — but carpet construction adds the real risk. Face yarn (nylon, olefin, polyester) is what the stain sits on; backing latex and pad underneath are what fail first if you over-wet. Pooled water or solvent sinks through the pile into the pad, where it can delaminate the carpet, grow mildew, and leave odor for weeks. Wool carpet is the fragile branch — enzymatic digestants that lift blood on cotton also attack wool keratin, and wool tolerates none of the same temperatures. Cold water + cloth-transfer + aggressive extraction is the rule.
whyThisWorks: Speed, cold water, and extraction between every wet pass. Fresh blood comes up with cold water + blotting if you catch it in the first 30 minutes. Residual stain gives way to enzymatic digestion on synthetic face yarn. The invisible failure mode is backing / pad damage from pooled liquid — transfer-from-cloth only. Wool carpet escalates early because the safe-for-cotton protocol is dangerous on wool.
defaultAssumption: Assume nylon or olefin face yarn unless the user says wool. Synthetic face yarn gives the widest chemistry latitude; wool carpet routes to a professional much earlier.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Ask: is the carpet wool, or is it synthetic (nylon, olefin, polyester)? Wool carpet + blood = call a pro now. Most enzyme digestants damage wool keratin, and wool tolerates none of the chlorine or high-peroxide steps that synthetic face yarn handles fine. Synthetic face yarn = proceed.

spottingProtocol.equipment: White terry towels, cold water spray bottle, wet/dry vacuum if available
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply cold water with a damp cloth to the stain — never pour or saturate. Press, lift, rotate to a clean section, repeat. After every 3–4 presses, switch to a DRY towel and press down hard (a weight or your knee) to pull moisture up and out. Extract with a wet/dry vacuum if you have one. Hemoglobin transfers onto the cloth. Two or three wet-then-dry cycles often clear a fresh stain entirely.

spottingProtocol.technique: Apply liquid to a cloth, transfer, extract
spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter or dissolved enzyme pretreat
spottingProtocol.instruction: Synthetic face yarn only. Apply liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or dissolved enzyme pretreat — never dry powder on carpet) to a damp cloth. Press onto the stain, lift, rotate, repeat. Let the applied spotter dwell 5–10 min. Extract aggressively with a dry weighted towel before the next step. Pool + pad = odor + mildew.

spottingProtocol.technique: Drops on cloth only, watch the shadow
spottingProtocol.instruction: For pink or brown shadow on synthetic face yarn: dab 3% hydrogen peroxide onto a clean white cloth — do not pour onto the carpet. Press onto the shadow. Watch closely. Peroxide will lighten the stain and in some cases lighten the face yarn color too — test a hidden section first, and flush the moment the shadow is gone.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Neutral Synthetic Detergent, wet/dry vac or heavy towels
spottingProtocol.instruction: Damp cloth with NSD, press onto the treated area to remove residual enzyme and peroxide. Extract thoroughly with a dry weighted towel. Residual chemistry in the pad is what causes long-term odor and mildew.

Weight a folded dry towel onto the spot for 30 min to pull residual moisture from the backing. Point a fan at the area. Air-dry fully before walking on it. Inspect for odor (pad contamination), tackiness (residual chemistry), or edge delamination. Any of those = specialist.

steps: 1. Fiber check: wool = escalate. Synthetic = proceed.
2. Cold water on damp cloth, press-and-lift, extract between passes with dry weighted towel or wet/dry vac.
3. Liquid Protein Spotter on damp cloth — never pour. Dwell 5–10 min. Extract.
4. For shadow: 3% H₂O₂ dabbed on a cloth only, never on the carpet. Test hidden area.
5. NSD cloth + aggressive extraction — chemistry residue in the pad causes odor.
6. Fan-dry + weighted towel 30 min. Inspect for odor, tackiness, delamination.
products: Liquid Protein Spotter (PRENETT B or equivalent)
Wet/dry vacuum or heavy absorbent white towels
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold through all steps — warm water activates backing adhesive
warnings: Never pool or pour liquid on carpet — sinks to backing and pad
Never use enzyme digestant on wool carpet — attacks wool keratin
Never skip extraction between wet passes — pad moisture causes odor + mildew
Never use hot water — activates backing adhesive + sets protein permanently
Test 3% H₂O₂ on a hidden section before treating the visible stain

homeSolutions.instruction: Is the carpet wool or synthetic? If wool (or "natural fiber blend"), call a carpet-cleaning pro now — wool is fragile and most blood treatments that work on synthetic carpet damage wool. Synthetic (nylon, olefin, polyester) = proceed with home treatment.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use enzyme pretreats on wool carpet — enzymes digest wool fiber.

homeSolutions.instruction: Fresh blood on synthetic carpet comes up with cold water + aggressive blotting, but you have minutes. Hot water cooks the protein AND can activate the carpet backing adhesive. Cold only.

Put cold water on a clean white cloth. Press onto the stain, lift, rotate to a clean section, repeat. Do NOT pour water onto the carpet — pooled liquid sinks through to the pad, and that's how you ruin the floor underneath. The cloth transfer is slow and deliberate; that's the right speed.

homeSolutions.agent: Extract with a Dry Weighted Towel
homeSolutions.instruction: After every 3–4 press-and-lift passes, switch to a thick dry towel. Press down hard — put a book or heavy pan on top for 1–2 minutes. The dry towel pulls moisture up and out of the pad. Check it — you'll often see blood on the dry towel too. Swap to a fresh section, repeat until the towel comes up clean.

homeSolutions.agent: Enzyme Pretreat — Dissolved, on a Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: If a shadow remains on synthetic carpet: dissolve an enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout) fully in cold water per the label. Apply to a cloth, press onto the stain, let dwell 10 minutes, then extract with a dry weighted towel. Never sprinkle powder on carpet — it works into the pile and is nearly impossible to remove.

For stubborn shadow on synthetic carpet: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide onto a clean white cloth — never pour on the carpet. Press onto the stain. Watch closely — peroxide can lighten the carpet face yarn too, so test a hidden corner first. Flush cool-water-on-cloth immediately when the shadow is gone.

homeSolutions.agent: Fan Dry, Don't Walk on It
homeSolutions.instruction: Point a fan at the spot. Leave a folded dry towel on it with something heavy for 30 min. Let the carpet air-dry fully before walking on the area — several hours. Walking on damp carpet pushes moisture into the pad and into the fibers you just cleaned.

homeSolutions.agent: Smell Check, Then Call a Pro
homeSolutions.instruction: Once dry, smell the spot. A musty or chemical smell means moisture reached the pad and you need a professional extraction. Tacky or crunchy pile means residual chemistry is still there. Tell the pro: "Blood on [synthetic/wool] carpet, age [time], home-treated with cold water + enzyme pretreat + 3% hydrogen peroxide, no pooling, no hot water."

steps: 1. Check carpet fiber: wool = call a pro. Synthetic = continue.
2. Cold water on a damp cloth, press-and-lift onto the stain. Never pour.
3. Extract every 3–4 passes with a dry weighted towel.
4. For shadow: dissolve enzyme pretreat in cold water, apply via cloth, dwell 10 min, extract.
5. For stubborn shadow: 3% hydrogen peroxide dabbed on a cloth only, test hidden area first.
6. Fan-dry fully before walking. Smell-check for musty odor = pro extraction.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout) dissolved in cold water
Thick dry towels + something heavy for extraction
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Wool carpet, musty smell after drying, tacky / crunchy pile, or two home rounds without clearing it — call a carpet-cleaning professional.
warnings: Never pour or pool liquid on carpet — damages backing and pad
Never use enzyme or peroxide on wool carpet — specialist territory
Never skip the dry-towel extraction between wet passes
Never use hot water — activates backing adhesive + sets protein

neverDo: Never pool or pour solvent or water on carpet — backing and pad damage
Never use enzyme digestant on wool carpet — attacks keratin
Never skip extraction between wet passes
Never use hot water on carpet with blood — cooks protein + loosens backing adhesive
fiberSensitivities: Synthetic face yarn (nylon, olefin, polyester): enzyme + peroxide safe
Wool carpet: enzyme-sensitive, peroxide-sensitive, heat-sensitive — escalate early
Backing latex: dissolves under pooled water or hot liquid — the invisible failure mode

materialWarnings: Transfer liquid from a cloth — never pour on carpet
Wool carpet + blood = professional carpet cleaner
Extract moisture between every wet pass

escalation.when: Wool carpet, musty / chemical odor after drying, tacky or crunchy pile after drying, blood older than 24 hours, or two home rounds without clearing it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Blood on [nylon / wool / synthetic] carpet, age [time], home-treated with cold water + enzyme pretreat + 3% hydrogen peroxide, no pooling, no hot water."

contextQuestions: What's the carpet fiber — nylon, olefin, polyester, wool, or unknown?
Any hot water or heat already applied?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Wool + blood = always pro. Synthetic + fresh = DIY. Backing/pad moisture is the invisible failure mode — cloth transfer + extraction, never pooling.
